summary.objective: The paper investigates a minimal chase-and-escape model on a two-dimensional square lattice with randomly distributed static obstacles to determine how geometric disorder controls collective pursuit dynamics, establishing a direct link between lattice connectivity and pursuit efficiency.
summary.methods: Using agent-based simulations with short-range sensing rules, they varied obstacle density and analyzed geometric accessibility via Breadth-First Search, trapping time, capture cost, survival statistics (Weibull distribution), and mean-squared displacement exponent.
summary.results: Results show non-monotonic dependence of trapping time and cost on obstacle density, reflecting competition between path elongation and geometric fragmentation. Survival analysis reveals Weibull decay with crossovers in characteristic time and shape parameters, while transport exhibits subdiffusive dynamics converging near the percolation threshold.
summary.conclusion: Overall, the dynamics are governed by a geometry-driven crossover where percolation and connectivity act as unifying control parameters for spatial, temporal, and collective behavior.
